title: What is CBT?
citation: Beck, A.T. (1979). Cognitive therapy of depression. Guilford Press.
content:
Cognitive behavioural therapy, commonly known as CBT, is a structured, evidence-based form of talking therapy that has been used since the 1970s to treat a wide range of mental health difficulties. At its core, CBT is built on a simple but powerful idea: our thoughts, feelings, and behaviours are closely connected, and changing the way we think about situations can shift how we feel and what we do as a result.

The approach was developed by psychiatrist Aaron Beck, who noticed that his patients with depression often held automatic, distorted patterns of thinking, such as seeing themselves, the world, and the future in unrealistically negative terms. Beck found that helping patients identify and examine these thoughts could produce meaningful improvements in mood and functioning.

In CBT, a therapist and client work together as a team. The therapist does not hand down answers or tell the client what to think. Instead, they help the client notice unhelpful thought patterns, question whether those thoughts are accurate or fair, and test out new ways of thinking and behaving in everyday life. This collaborative relationship is one of the defining features of CBT and distinguishes it from more directive approaches.

Sessions are structured and typically agenda-driven. At the start of each session, the therapist and client agree on what to focus on. Between sessions, the client usually completes exercises, such as keeping a thought diary or trying a specific behavioural task. These between-session activities, sometimes called homework, are central to CBT because real change happens through practice in daily life, not only inside the therapy room.

CBT is time-limited in design. A course of treatment usually runs between six and twenty sessions, depending on the nature and complexity of the difficulty being addressed. It is one of the most thoroughly researched psychological treatments available, with a strong evidence base across anxiety disorders, depression, trauma, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and many other conditions.
